1.synthesis: Psalm 111 is a 22-line alphabetic acrostic (each half-line beginning with the next Hebrew letter), a form that signals completeness: this is the full praise of YHWH’s works “from A to Z.” Its opening sets the mode: “I will give thanks to the Lord with my whole heart, in the company of the upright, in the congregation” (v. 1). The great works of YHWH are to be darash—studied, sought out (v. 2)—by all who delight in them. He has established a zeker (memorial) for his wondrous works (v. 4); the Passover and Sabbath are institutional memorials of this kind. His work is emet u-mishpat (truth and justice, v. 7), and he has sent geulah (redemption) to his people (v. 9). The wisdom conclusion is near-verbatim Proverbs 9:10: “The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom; all those who practice it have a good understanding” (v. 10).
1.historical_context: Psalm 111 and Psalm 112 are twin acrostic psalms, deliberately paired as companion pieces: 111 praises YHWH’s character and works, while 112 describes the character of the one who fears YHWH. The verbal echoes between them are exact: what verse 3 ascribes to YHWH (“his righteousness endures forever”) is ascribed in Psalm 112:3 to the righteous person—a deliberate theological statement that the God-fearer comes to reflect YHWH’s own character. The geulah (redemption) of verse 9 points primarily to the Exodus but carries an eschatological horizon. The near-verbatim overlap with Proverbs 9:10 situates Psalm 111 firmly in the wisdom tradition.
1.literary_structure: The 22 half-lines of Psalm 111 progress from the act of praise (v. 1) through the character of YHWH’s works (vv. 2–8) to their covenant context (vv. 9) to the wisdom conclusion (v. 10). The works themselves have a double character throughout: they are great and splendid (v. 3), but they are also studied and delighted in by the congregation (v. 2). The zeker (memorial) established for them (v. 4) means YHWH’s past works are not archived history but living liturgical memory. The form itself—the complete alphabet—enacts what the content describes: a complete, orderly, comprehensive account of YHWH’s faithfulness.
1.theological_application: Psalm 111 models congregational study (darash, v. 2) as an act of worship. To study YHWH’s works is not a merely intellectual exercise but a mode of honoring him, done “in the congregation” where that honor becomes public. The wisdom conclusion (v. 10) frames the entire psalm: genuine reverence for YHWH begins (“the beginning of wisdom”) with a right apprehension of who he is and what he has done. The darash of verse 2 is also the word for the interpretive study of Torah: Psalm 111 thus links the study of history (what YHWH has done) and Torah (what YHWH has commanded) as inseparable dimensions of wisdom.
1.christology: The geulah (redemption) sent to YHWH’s people (v. 9) and the “holy and awesome” name that accompanies it point forward to the one who is himself the redemption YHWH sends. Luke 1:68 (“he has visited and redeemed his people”) echoes the vocabulary of Psalm 111 in announcing the birth of Jesus. In Colossians 2:3, Paul writes that in Christ “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge”—the hokhmah (wisdom) that Psalm 111:10 promises as the fruit of fearing YHWH is concentrated and embodied in Christ, who “became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption” (1 Cor 1:30).
